speaker: interviewer
text: Alex Chevion interview, tape six.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we leave at about 8 o'clock at night. We leave. We leave with the boat. Everything seems OK up until about 1 o'clock at night or maybe 2 o'clock at night-- rather, 2 o'clock at night. Suddenly, we hear some strange voices from a distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: At first, people start theorizing, sounds like a gypsy music. But we could be Gypsy music? But then on the other side of the Black Sea, there is Georgia. It may be some Georgian tribes which, you know, [INAUDIBLE]. And then before we know, we hear people's voices around the boat. Oh, my god-- who are those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we look around. And the seas are sort of lightly swellings, that they try to reach our boat but they can't reach our boat. So we throw ropes. One guy manages to catch the rope and pull him up. He gets up on the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he tells us our ship was just bombarded by a submarine and torpedoed. And as a matter of fact, he said they sent-- after the ship was already sinking, they kept shooting at us, you know? And we stand there. And our captain of the boat suddenly is running like crazy, like possessed. And he wouldn't let us throw these ropes to those people, because he's afraid that the-- and then light is just breaking. It's dawn. Suddenly, light is just breaking. And at a distance, some people claim that they saw the silhouette of submarine. And here are those people swimming, you know? And here is that captain. We had to arrest the captain, grab him, see, overpower him, and take away the gun from him, and put him into a temporary cell there, you know, hold him. And then we kept throwing those ropes. We managed to save nine people only from those. One of them was a woman who was in the ninth month. And she gave birth by the time we arrived to Aleppo. Now, if I can, I have a little excerpt from the book. You don't want me to show that? Not now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So these people were saved, nine of them only. So out of the three ships, Marina--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names--

speaker: interviewee
text: Marina managed to reach Turkey simply without any hitch. The [NON-ENGLISH] was torpedoed by a German submarine. And nine survivors from that managed to reach my boat, which was called [NON-ENGLISH]. Now we are at [NON-ENGLISH]. And suddenly, after that incidence, after that horrible story with the nine survivors, barely survivors, the sea swelled and a terrible storm ensued. And instead of reaching Istanbul, where we were heading, we found ourselves in Asiatic Turkey, not knowing that it's Turkey. For what we knew, could have been Bulgaria. See, the storm was so violent. We had sails. It was basically a sailboat with a small outboard motor. The sails got broken. The compass got wrecked. The whole ship got wrecked. It took like, oh-- the seas were throwing us in all directions for about 10 days. Before, we're supposed to be in a day in Turkey. And here it was 10 days later, and we were someplace. And we didn't know even where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the, the boat was loaded with food. But who could eat even? But anyhow, we stand there, not sign in sight, until about the third day. Thanks God, this boat appears to look at us. And the guy sees it. As he came close enough, he saw a boat full of people. He turns around and run away. He runs away. And he alerted, apparently, the police. And the police comes and starts talk to us. And we tell him. And he says you turn around. And you go away. We won't let you in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we tell him, no way, you see? And finally-- it was all in French. Somebody who spoke French on the boat conducted this whole dialogue. She managed to persuade them that all we ask is a favor, just contact the Jewish agency and-- or the Jewish community, whatever. Romanian Jews knew a little more about Turkish Jews. Contact this and that in Istanbul and tell them that we arrived. OK. Another three, four days passed by. He comes back, says, the authorities told me to tell you you will never be allowed to enter land. Turn around and go back. At that point, we had a little session amongst ourselves. And we all agreed that we take the outboard motor and throw it into the seas. That's the first we do, so that no way can that boat move. Either die here, or else they let us on land, see? And then ensued negotiations again. And they talk in Istanbul. And finally, they had no choice. They could either let us starve there, let us die and have us on our conscience. They agreed to let us in. And we disembarked from the boat, you see? It was pretty shallow already. We could walk. I mean, the boat just reached-- hit the land almost. And we started walking and then with donkeys and this. It took a long time. It probably took a month until we reached Istanbul. But we went by truck and by, you know, by, by foot, You know? Did the group stay together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stayed together-- it was like there was 200 of us on that boat. And we were all-- we were under police escort in a way, you know, because we were sort of illegal. And we reached Istanbul. And then finally, in Istanbul, they gave us papers. So we got papers that we were allowed to board the train and go towards Israel. But we didn't get permission from the British to enter Israel. We some sort got a temporary arrangements. So we traveled by train all across Turkey. The train was a shipment of melons, of watermelons. And we ate all the time watermelons. We all got sick from the watermelons. [LAUGHS] We finally reached the border of Syria, which is a long ride. Turkey, Asiatic Turkey is maybe, I don't know, 800 miles or 1,000 miles. It's quite a distance. And we went by that train. You know, train would stop on the way and stop and go. And we reached Syria and Aleppo. And then the British army took us over as their prisoners. And from there on, we went again to Atlit in Israel, which was a jail, a prison, a British camp for, for, for, you know, for prisoners. And that's, that's how I entered Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I entered Israel in August-- best I can remember-- no, that was August 2nd, I entered the ship. And the reported on the trip. It was September 17th they reported on it. So, so I must have reached Israel October 2nd, I'm guessing, but within two probably. October 2nd, I reached Atlit.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944. And in Atlit, I was about two weeks released. October 15th, became a free man. And I registered in the Technion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father? What about him? About

speaker: interviewee
text: Father with me was all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you settled then in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school for four years. And then the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, before we got to that, what about the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my mother and my sister lived together. My brother lived separately. And they were all in Warsaw. First, my mother and my sister were in Lwow. But then they also moved to Warsaw. My brother-- I wasn't in Poland anymore when they came to Warsaw. And they lived. And they were separate. They didn't know from each other for a long time. And Warsaw underwent a terrible siege, where the Russians were on one side and the Germans were on the other side. And there was a terrible fight there going on and terrible devastation. And they didn't know about if each other survive for like two, three months, up until after the war, where they met up together again. And they all survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find them? And how did you get reunited?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we found word after the war. The war ended in May of 1945. So we started to write to Poland, Red Cross, and announcements, and all that. Finally, we reached them somehow towards the end of 1945. We reached. We made contact, mostly that my mother knew my uncle. My uncle who lived in Israel in 1920. And my mother knew his address. And she wrote to him. And she told us she survived and all that. So we made contact sometimes in, say, July or so of 1945. And in 1946, my mother arrived in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your sister and brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: With sister, with my sister. My father and my brother remained in Poland. And he moved to Belgium from Poland. And later, in 1950, he came to the United States and lived in Brooklyn and died in 1967.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the reunion-- your family reunion in Israel after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, It was a great reunion. You know, my father was, without my mother, he was lost. And when she arrived, it was like new life started for him. And of course, I-- my mother loved me very much. She was very happy to see me. And things were nice. Life was good. And then one day in August of 1950, it was the last night I remember I was home, just finishing a big project for, for my work at the Technion, working all the time. And my mother was on the porch, very happy, in very good mood. She went to sleep. She never got up. She died.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: From after the war, or actually, '44, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, that was-- when my mother died was one year after-- it was-- the war finished in '49. The war started in '48.

speaker: interviewee
text: It finished in '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in the Israeli army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the Israeli army. But I was in the unit-- which I didn't chose, I was designated-- was a unit which was, it was called the research division of the army. And we were supposed to improvise arms. The, the-- Israel was under embargo. United States wouldn't sell it arms. France wouldn't sell-- France would sell some arms. But mostly, Czechoslovakia was selling arms. In other words, the British managed to convince everybody to impose an embargo on Israel arms. And strangely enough, this country is now such a great supporter of Israel, acquiesced in that pressure and refused to sell arms to Israel. And here, they had this war to fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this unit was charged with improvising. I mean, as fast as you can. And I was specifically assigned to working out bombs which could be dropped from Piper Cubs. You know these small airplanes? Just, the pilot would just simply take and shove out these bombs, small bombs. And I worked on that and was very successful. That proved a great asset for the army. And later, I worked on mortars, six-inch mortars. Again, you see, a six-inch mortar, the way it's normally done is done from special steels. What we did, we took a regular six-inch pipe but wrapped a steel wire around it, you know? So it was things like that. And-- And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's about it. And after the war finished, I went back to the Technion to complete my studies. But first, I went-- in my fourth year in the Technion, one of the professors organized a trip to Europe. He was, before the war, a renowned German expert. He had a lot of contacts. He was well-known Switzerland. And they invited him and invited us, a group of 20 students. And they gave us the time our life. They received us and wined us and dined us and showed us the industry. It was a great educational project. And everywhere, they honored him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after that trip, being that my brother lived in Belgium, I decided to take a little vacation. I stayed for a year in Europe. And I stayed with my brother. And then I moved to France a little bit and spent a year in Europe. And then I came back to Israel and completed my studies, just got a diploma and everything. As soon as I finished my studies, I went to the United States. Did you meet your wife in the United States or in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In April 2nd of 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Paula Rothstein from home Chevion.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you change your name to Chevion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I changed my name. Well, I'm officially-- I actually changed my name probably-- and I don't remember exactly officially-- must have been in 19-- when I came back from Europe-- must have been in-- before I received my diploma, which must have been in 1951. At that time, there was sort of-- Ben-Gurion was agitating very strong that the Jews should not have a German name. After what happened, the Jews should not have a German name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those Jews which are German then should change them to Hebrew name. My uncle happens to have that Hebrew name, Chevion. I changed it to Chevion. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And before that, my uncle always called me Chevion. So my aunts were also Chevion. So I kind of drifted into that name slowly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got married in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Got married in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what motivated you to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I studied engineering. And of course, at that time in the '50s, this was a great era for a lot of developments-- computers, ballistic or guidance, tape records, what have you. This was a great innovative area. And all these innovations were coming from the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anybody who dreamed about, you know, this type of stuff wanted to be in the United States. So I came here first sort of to study. But you know, this time, I stayed. And tell me about your family, your family. Your children were born here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Children were born here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have one daughter. Her name is Debbie. And she has a daughter, our granddaughter. Her name is Sarah. And my wife has a large family here in the United Sates. Because her mothers two brothers came here at the turn of the century, way back then. And they now have the third generation. So at this point, when we have, you know, bar mitzvahs, weddings, or parties, or whatever, or last night, after Yom Kippur, after breaking the fast, there is usually lots of people. So, so she has a very large family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your wife born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in Tarnopol, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now that the war is a distant but vivid memory, what message do you have for your daughter or for the world--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well.

speaker: interviewer
text: About your experience? How has it affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would think, I would think that probably to me, the most strange thing about the war is the fact that people refuse to believe that the Holocaust can happen, will happen, or is happening. In other words, my grandmother, when she was taken away from Tarnow, when she was taken away, she told to my sister, well, we'll survive. Never mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're just taking-- they are telling us that to a labor camp, you know? The Germans are saying, [GERMAN SPEECH,] which means work makes you happy. And so what? So I'll work and all that. In other words, nobody imagined or believed that this type of an atrocity, the Holocaust, can happen. So probably, the most important message to guard against this happening is to be conscious and aware of it and spread the word to people. Because yesterday, it happened to the Jews. Tomorrow, it can happen who knows who.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody ever believed or imagined that the whole nation can be taken and exterminated. Later, we saw in the Serbia and in other places in the world that things like this can happen. So probably the most important message that I feel we should do is keep telling that story. The Holocaust Museum is a great accomplishment. Spielberg's Foundation is a great accomplishments. And more and more, we should keep telling them. Because that way, you can guard against it. As people become aware and conscious, they can guard. They can do something about it. If the Polish Jews would have believed in the '30s that something like this can happen, something would have done at that time where they could still do something. Later, it's too late. There comes a point, both in Germany and in Poland, where you couldn't get a gun. You couldn't organize.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you were trying to organize-- you know the story of Heydrich? Heydrich was assassinated in a town in Lednice in Czechoslovakia. The whole village was erased.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tarnow, if I were to talk to a friend and tell him, look, let's get a gun just in case one day we will-- not I would have been killed. My whole family, 100 other Jews surrounding me would have been killed. At that point, it's too late to act.

speaker: interviewer
text: So instead, awareness, which this is my message basically. That's the only way you can guard. Because you can't foresee the future. You can't predict. And even so, many people feel it will never happen in this country. I agree. But who knows? But I keep adding, who knows?

speaker: interviewee
text: You never know. But if you learn about the Holocaust, then you are aware that this happened once. That provides a certain measure of safety and security.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the family-- a family picture. It was taken in honor of my uncle who came for a short visit from Israel. The picture was taken in the late '20s or maybe early '30s. And my uncle, who's visiting from Israel, is the top row on the left. His name is Meir Chevion. Next to him is another uncle. His name is Efraim [INAUDIBLE] Orbach. Next to him is my father. Next to him is--

speaker: interviewer
text: His, his name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Chune Fessel Chevion. Next to him is mine aunt named Reisel. The second row left is Blima Orbach, is a cousin. Her mother, Reisel Orbach, my grandmother on my mother's side. Her name is Devorah Wilk, my mother, whose name is Miriam Wilk Fessel. The last row on the left is my brother, Peretz Fessel. Next to him is me, myself. Next to me is a cousin. His name is Peretz Orbach. Next to him is my sister, Blima Chava-- now Chava Gilad. Next to her is a cousin from Israel, which is Meir's daughter. Her name is Tamar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in Tarnow.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland. This picture was taken in Tarnow. It's a family picture. It was taken about 1932. On the right, on the bottom right, my father. Next to him is my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is Chune Fessel. Next to him is my sister, Blima Fessel, now Gilad. Next to her is my mother, Miriam Wilk Fessel. Next above is myself. And next to me is my brother, Peretz Fessel. That's the name of the factory. It's a letterhead of the factory that my father owned that during the war was working for the Germans. And I worked there. My mother worked there. My brother were there until we all left and escaped Tarnow. But so long we, we were able to work there, we fulfilled the requirements that the Jews had to work for some sort of an institutions, generally for a German institution. And the factory was owned by a German during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This particular letter is a letterhead which was already printed after the war by the-- it says that it is under the administration of the Polish government. In other words, that factory was nationalized by the Polish government after the war. My mother brought it with her when she came to Israel. This is a school picture. And I believe it's a graduation picture, which I am then in 7th grade, which must be 1930-- take it back. No. It's-- I'm here, oh, I would guess about eight years old, which means it was taken in 1932. It's not yet graduation. Now I am on the left, bottom row on the left. The top row are my teachers. On the left is Mr. Glasser, who taught Hebrew subjects. Next to him is Mrs. Getzler. She taught mathematics. Next to her is the principal. His name is Mr. Malkeshur. Next to him is Mr. Laibel. He taught Talmud and Torah and Tanakh. This is a picture taken right after the war, end of 1945 or beginning of 1946. I am a student in the Technion in the chemistry department doing chem work, chem lab assignments in the chemistry department, most likely analytical chemistry, trying to discover whether the solution that I was given contains which metal and by what name and so forth. This is our wedding picture, mine and my wife's. This is taken in 1952 at the wedding. At the time, I am 28 years old. My wife is 24 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Paula Chevion. And that's me, Alex Chevion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Haifa. The wedding was in Haifa in the courtyard of a rabbi who performed the wedding. This is my daughter's picture after her wedding, immediately after her wedding, with us. It was taken in September of 1991. And next to the right is my daughter, of course, after her wedding. This is my granddaughter, Sarah, Sarah Chevion Muller. She was born December 26, 1995. She's very happy to be alive. And I'm happy, too, that she is. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: She's still happy baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Paula, next to me. I just finished telling you my story. I hope my granddaughter and my future grandchildren which I will have, one day, probably 20 years from today when they will be old enough to read and understand, will have an insight that what happened in this time about the great tragedy that befell the Jewish people, the Holocaust.
